---
bibliography: references.bib
---

# The Problem

## The spatial data bottleneck in R

The dominant spatial workflow in R — using **{sf}** [@sfbook, @sf] — requires all data to be fully loaded into RAM before any operation can take place. For datasets containing millions of geometries (e.g., global
road networks, buildings), this forces users into slow workarounds such as tiling, chunking, or offloading to external tools.

Beyond memory, {sf} introduces serialization overhead for bulk operations: most geometric engines require WKB roundtrips to ingest data from R, incurring computational costs at every pipeline step. The
**{geos}** [@geos] package partially mitigates this via C-level structures, but remains a geometry library rather than a spatial data engine — it does not support out-of-core processing or relational spatial
joins on massive datasets.

Neither {sf} nor {geos} support reading `.parquet` files natively. GeoParquet is rapidly becoming the standard format for large-scale vector data, but its full potential remains inaccessible to R users.

## The missing piece: DuckDB as a spatial backend

DuckDB [@duckdb] executes complex queries on disk-resident data with minimal memory overhead. Its spatial extension supports predicates, transformations, buffers, and spatial joins. The H3 extension adds
native support for Uber's hexagonal indexing system, widely used in mobility analysis, ecological modelling, and spatial aggregation.

Despite these capabilities, no first-class R interface exists. Users must write raw SQL or accept workarounds that materialise data into R memory anyway.

## Existing packages and their limitations

- **{sf}**, **{geos}** — industry standard but eager; all data must be in RAM.
- **{duckplyr}** [@duckplyr], **{dbplyr}** [@dbplyr] — lazy DuckDB evaluation, but no spatial type support.
- **{sfarrow}** [@sfarrow], **{geoarrow}** [@geoarrow] — Arrow-based spatial I/O, but no analytical query layer.
- **{h3jsr}** [@h3jsr], **{h3o}** [@h3o] — H3 indexing in R, but entirely in memory.

No existing package connects R's spatial ecosystem to DuckDB in a lazy, memory-efficient, and idiomatic way.

## What solving this enables

**{duckspatial}** and **{duckh3}** fill this gap with a lazy spatial data frame class (`duckspatial_df`) that pushes computation into DuckDB, returning results to R only when explicitly requested:

- Analysis of datasets exceeding available RAM with no tiling required.
- Geometry operations and spatial joins on multi-gigabyte files from disk.
- Large-scale H3 aggregation integrated with the same lazy workflow.
- Familiar **{sf}**-style names so existing users can adopt without relearning an API.
